description: Email templates for Emails Please. 10 legitimate, 10 phishing, 10 spam. Used as fallback when API is unavailable.

emails.subject: Mandatory Password Policy Update — Action Required by Friday
emails.body:
Dear Team,

Effective this Friday, all employee passwords must meet our updated security requirements:

- Minimum 12 characters
- At least one uppercase letter, number, and symbol
- Cannot reuse your last 5 passwords

Please update your password by logging into the company portal at intranet.malwareinc.com and navigating to Account Settings.

Contact the helpdesk at ext. 4400 if you need assistance.

Thank you,
IT Support Team
emails.hint: Legitimate IT emails direct you to internal company portals, not external links. The sender domain matches the company and contact info is provided.

emails.subject: 2005 Benefits Enrollment Now Open
emails.body:
Hi everyone,

Open enrollment for 2005 employee benefits begins today and closes March 28th. This is your opportunity to review and update your health, dental, vision, and 401(k) elections.

To make changes, log into the HR portal at hr.malwareinc.com using your employee ID.

If you have questions, contact HR directly at hr@malwareinc.com or call ext. 2200.

Best regards,
Sarah Mitchell
Human Resources
emails.hint: Legitimate HR emails use internal portals and company email addresses. Deadlines are reasonable and contact information is provided.

emails.subject: Team Sync — Thursday 2:00 PM, Conference Room B
emails.body:
Hi team,

Reminder that our weekly sync is this Thursday at 2:00 PM in Conference Room B.

Agenda:
1. Q1 project status updates
2. Client feedback from last week's demo
3. Upcoming deadlines for April

Please come prepared with a 2-minute update on your current tasks. If you cannot attend, let me know in advance.

See you Thursday,
David Park
Senior Project Manager
emails.hint: Internal meeting invites come from known colleagues with company email addresses and include specific location details.

emails.subject: Invoice #4821 Requires Your Approval
emails.body:
Hello,

Invoice #4821 from Contoso Supplies ($3,240.00) has been submitted for your approval.

Please review and approve or reject it by logging into the finance portal at finance.malwareinc.com > Pending Approvals.

If you have questions about this invoice, contact Accounts Payable at ap@malwareinc.com or ext. 3300.

Thank you,
Accounts Payable Team
emails.hint: Finance departments route approvals through internal portals and include invoice numbers and vendor details. No credential requests are made.

emails.subject: Scheduled Maintenance — Sunday 2:00–6:00 AM
emails.body:
Dear All,

We will be performing scheduled server maintenance this Sunday, March 20th, from 2:00 AM to 6:00 AM.

The following services will be unavailable during this window:
- Email
- VPN access
- The finance portal

Please plan accordingly. There is no action required from you.

We apologize for the inconvenience.

IT Operations Team
emails.hint: Maintenance notices are informational, require no action, and come from known IT addresses. No links or login requests are included.

emails.subject: NDA for Project Orion — Please Review by EOD Friday
emails.body:
Hello,

Please find attached the Non-Disclosure Agreement for Project Orion. Legal has reviewed the document and it is ready for your signature.

Please review and return the signed copy to legal@malwareinc.com by end of business this Friday.

If you have any questions or require changes, contact the Legal team at ext. 5500.

Thank you,
Legal Department
emails.hint: Internal legal requests come from company domains and ask for signatures via established processes, not external sites.

emails.subject: Office Closed — Good Friday, March 25th
emails.body:
Hi everyone,

This is a reminder that the office will be closed on Friday, March 25th in observance of Good Friday. Normal office hours resume Monday, March 28th.

If you need to access the building over the holiday weekend, please contact Security at ext. 1100 at least 24 hours in advance.

Have a great long weekend!

Facilities Management
emails.hint: Office closure notices are informational and require no action. They come from known internal departments with no external links.

emails.subject: Your Q1 Performance Review — Scheduled for March 22nd
emails.body:
Hi,

I've scheduled your Q1 performance review for Tuesday, March 22nd at 10:00 AM in my office (Room 214).

Please take a few minutes beforehand to reflect on:
- Goals you set at the start of the quarter
- Achievements and challenges
- Development areas for Q2

Looking forward to our conversation.

David Park
Senior Project Manager
emails.hint: Performance review notices come from a known manager's email and include specific room and time details.

emails.subject: Your Microsoft Office License Renewed — No Action Needed
emails.body:
Hello,

This is a confirmation that your Microsoft Office license has been automatically renewed for the 2005–2006 year. No action is required on your part.

Your software will continue to work without interruption. If you experience any issues, please contact the helpdesk at ext. 4400.

Thank you,
IT Department
emails.hint: Legitimate license renewal notices are informational and explicitly state no action is needed. No login links are included.

emails.subject: Welcome — New Team Member Starting Monday
emails.body:
Hi team,

Please join me in welcoming Alex Rivera, who will be joining us as a Junior Developer starting this Monday, March 21st.

Alex comes to us from Northstar Solutions and brings 3 years of experience in web development. Please make them feel welcome!

We'll have a brief team introduction at 9:30 AM Monday in the main conference room.

Best,
Sarah Mitchell
Human Resources
emails.hint: Welcome emails come from HR addresses, introduce specific people, and include verifiable details like start dates and meeting times.

emails.subject: URGENT: Your Account Has Been Suspended — Verify Now
emails.body:
Dear Valued Customer,

We have detected suspicious activity on your Bank of America account. Your account has been temporarily suspended to protect you.

To restore access, you must verify your identity within 24 hours or your account will be permanently closed.

Click here to verify: http://bankofamerica-secure-alerts.net/verify

Bank of America Security Team
redFlags: Sender domain is 'bankofamerica-secure-alerts.net' — not the real bankofamerica.com
Creates extreme urgency with a 24-hour deadline
Threatens permanent account closure
Link goes to an external non-bank domain
Generic greeting 'Dear Valued Customer'
emails.hint: Real banks will never ask you to verify account details by clicking an email link. Always go directly to your bank's website by typing the address yourself.

emails.subject: Your PayPal Account Has Been Limited
emails.body:
Dear PayPal User,

We noticed unusual sign-in activity from an unrecognized device. Your account has been limited until we can verify your information.

Please confirm your details within 48 hours:
http://paypa1-accounts.com/confirm

Failure to verify will result in permanent account suspension.

PayPal Account Services
redFlags: Sender domain is 'paypa1-accounts.com' — the '1' replaces the letter 'l' in PayPal
Generic greeting 'Dear PayPal User'
Threatens permanent suspension
External link not from paypal.com
Urgency pressure with 48-hour window
emails.hint: Look carefully at the sender's email domain. Attackers use lookalike domains like 'paypa1' (with a number 1) to impersonate trusted companies.

emails.subject: Tax Refund of $1,847.00 Pending — Claim Within 72 Hours
emails.body:
NOTICE FROM THE INTERNAL REVENUE SERVICE:

After a review of your 2004 federal tax return, you are eligible for a refund of $1,847.00.

To receive your refund, you must submit your banking information through our secure portal within 72 hours. After this window, the refund will be forfeited.

Claim your refund: http://irs-gov-refunds.com/claim

Internal Revenue Service
Refund Processing Division
redFlags: The IRS does not contact taxpayers by email — only by mail
Sender domain is 'irs-gov-refunds.com' — not irs.gov
Creates urgency with a 72-hour forfeiture deadline
Asks for banking information via email link
Unusually specific refund amount to seem credible
emails.hint: The IRS only contacts taxpayers by postal mail. They will never email you asking for bank details or threatening to forfeit your refund.

emails.subject: Unusual Sign-In Detected — Secure Your Account Now
emails.body:
Dear Microsoft User,

We detected a sign-in to your Microsoft account from:
Location: Kyiv, Ukraine
Device: Unknown Windows PC
Time: 03/15/2005 14:18 UTC

If this was not you, your account may be compromised. Secure it immediately:
http://microsoft-alerts.org/secure-account

If you do not act within 12 hours, your account will be locked for security purposes.

Microsoft Security Team
redFlags: Sender domain is 'microsoft-alerts.org' — not microsoft.com
Fabricated location detail to create panic
12-hour deadline creates false urgency
Link does not go to microsoft.com
Generic 'Dear Microsoft User' greeting
emails.hint: Microsoft security alerts come from microsoft.com addresses. Verify any security alert by going directly to account.microsoft.com — never click email links.

emails.subject: Urgent Wire Transfer Needed — Confidential
emails.body:
Hi,

I'm currently in a board meeting and need your immediate help. We need to complete a confidential wire transfer of $14,500 to a new vendor today.

Please process this to the following account:
Bank: First National
Routing: 021000021
Account: 7834920011

This is time-sensitive. Please handle immediately and do not discuss with others until it is complete. I will explain more later.

Thanks,
Robert Haines
CEO
redFlags: Sender domain is 'company-corp.net' — not the real company domain
Requests urgent wire transfer with no prior discussion
Asks for secrecy — 'do not discuss with others'
Pressure to act immediately without verification
Classic Business Email Compromise (BEC) pattern
emails.hint: This is a Business Email Compromise (BEC) attack impersonating a CEO. Always verify wire transfer requests by phone using a known number — never process based on email alone.

emails.subject: Your Email Account Will Be Deactivated
emails.body:
Dear Employee,

Our records show your email account has exceeded its storage quota and will be deactivated within 24 hours unless you verify your credentials.

Please confirm your username and password by clicking below:
http://company-it-support.net/email-verify

Failure to verify will result in loss of access to all company systems.

IT Helpdesk
redFlags: Legitimate IT will never ask for your password by email
Sender domain does not match the real company domain
Threatens loss of access to create urgency
Asks you to click an external link and enter credentials
Generic 'Dear Employee' greeting
emails.hint: No legitimate IT department will ever ask for your password via email. This is a credential harvesting attack.

emails.subject: You Have a Document Waiting for Your Signature
emails.body:
DocuSign Electronic Signature

Hello,

Robert Haines has sent you a document to review and sign.

DOCUMENT: Salary_Adjustment_Q2_2005.pdf

PLEASE SIGN: http://docusign-document-ready.com/sign/8f3k2

This link expires in 48 hours.

Do Not Share This Email — This message was sent to you by DocuSign on behalf of Robert Haines.
redFlags: Sender domain is 'docusign-document-ready.com' — real DocuSign uses docusign.net or docusign.com
Enticing subject about a salary adjustment to make you curious
No company context or prior communication
External link not from docusign.com or docusign.net
emails.hint: DocuSign sends from docusign.net or docusign.com. Always hover over links before clicking to check the real destination domain.

emails.subject: Someone Tried to Access Your LinkedIn Account
emails.body:
Hi,

We noticed someone tried to sign in to your LinkedIn account from an unrecognized device.

If this wasn't you, reset your password immediately to protect your account:
http://linkedin-account-alerts.com/reset

If you don't reset within 24 hours, your account may be compromised.

The LinkedIn Team
redFlags: Sender domain is 'linkedin-account-alerts.com' — not linkedin.com
Creates panic about an unauthorized access attempt
Link does not go to linkedin.com
24-hour deadline creates false urgency
Generic greeting with no name
emails.hint: Real LinkedIn security emails come from linkedin.com addresses. Go directly to linkedin.com to check your account security settings.

emails.subject: Your Amazon Order #114-8823901 Has Shipped
emails.body:
Hello Customer,

Your recent Amazon order has shipped!

Order #114-8823901
Item: Sony Handycam DCR-HC20 (x1)
Estimated delivery: March 22-24

Track your package: http://arnazon-shipping.com/track/8823901

If you did not place this order, click here to cancel and secure your account:
http://arnazon-shipping.com/cancel

Amazon Customer Service
redFlags: Sender domain is 'arnazon-shipping.com' — 'rn' looks like 'm' at a glance
Uses urgency of an unexpected order to provoke a click
Tracking and cancel links both go to the fake domain
Generic 'Hello Customer' — Amazon uses your name
Amazon order emails come from amazon.com, not third-party domains
emails.hint: Amazon always addresses you by your full name. Check the sender domain carefully — 'arnazon' uses 'rn' to mimic the letter 'm'.

emails.subject: Robert Haines Shared a File With You
emails.body:
Google Drive

Robert Haines (r.haines@company.com) has shared the following file with you:

Q2_Salary_Review_Confidential.xlsx

OPEN IN GOOGLE DRIVE: http://google-docs-shared.com/open/q2review

You need to sign in with your Google account to view this file.
redFlags: Sender domain is 'google-docs-shared.com' — not google.com or drive.google.com
Enticing confidential salary file to provoke curiosity
Link does not go to drive.google.com
Asks you to sign in — credential harvesting
Reuses a familiar name to seem trustworthy
emails.hint: Google Drive sharing emails come from google.com. The share link will always go to drive.google.com — never a third-party domain.

emails.subject: Lose 30 Pounds in 30 Days — Guaranteed or Your Money Back!
emails.body:
DOCTORS HATE HIM!

A Wisconsin man discovered a secret weight loss trick that Big Pharma doesn't want you to know.

Our revolutionary SlimBlast formula melts fat OVERNIGHT while you sleep. No diet. No exercise. 100% natural.

LIMITED TIME: Buy 2 bottles, get 3 FREE!

Order now at slimfast-solutions-deals.biz/order

This offer expires TONIGHT at midnight.

To unsubscribe reply STOP.
redFlags: Unrealistic health claims — lose 30 pounds in 30 days
Conspiracy language — 'doctors hate him', 'Big Pharma'
Fake scarcity with a midnight expiration
Sender uses a .biz domain with a hyphenated name
Unsolicited commercial email
emails.hint: Spam often uses extreme health claims, conspiracy language, and artificial scarcity. No legitimate product guarantees weight loss without diet or exercise.

emails.subject: CONGRATULATIONS — You Have Won $850,000.00!
emails.body:
CONGRATULATIONS!

You have been selected as the winner of the MEGA INTERNATIONAL LOTTERY 2005!

PRIZE: $850,000.00 USD

Your email was randomly chosen from 10,000,000 entries worldwide. To claim your prize, send the following to our claims office:

- Full name
- Address
- Phone number
- Copy of photo ID

Contact: claims@mega-lottery-intl.org
Ref: MLI-2005-8847

Congratulations again!
redFlags: You cannot win a lottery you did not enter
Asks for personal information including photo ID
Vague 'claims office' with no verifiable address
Sender uses .org domain for a supposed lottery
Classic advance-fee fraud setup
emails.hint: You cannot win a lottery you never entered. This is a data harvesting scam — sending your ID will lead to identity theft.

emails.subject: Strictly Confidential Business Proposal — $24.5 Million USD
emails.body:
Dearest Friend,

I am Prince Adebayo Okafor, son of the late General Okafor of Nigeria. Following my father's passing, I have inherited $24.5 million USD which I must transfer out of the country urgently.

I require a trusted foreign partner to help facilitate this transfer. In return you will receive 30% ($7.35 million) as compensation.

This is entirely legal and confidential. Please reply with your full name, bank details, and phone number to begin the process.

God Bless,
Prince Adebayo Okafor
redFlags: The classic 'Nigerian Prince' advance-fee fraud (419 scam)
Unsolicited contact from a stranger claiming enormous wealth
Requests bank account details
Sent from a free Gmail account
Promises unrealistically large reward for minimal effort
emails.hint: The 'Nigerian Prince' scam is one of the oldest internet frauds. If you share bank details, you will eventually be asked to pay 'fees' before receiving money that never arrives.

emails.subject: Prescription Meds — 80% Off! No Prescription Needed
emails.body:
Save BIG on your medications!

CanadianPharmacyDirect.biz offers:
- Viagra: $1.20/pill
- Ambien: $0.80/pill
- OxyContin: $1.50/pill
- Lipitor: $0.40/pill

NO PRESCRIPTION REQUIRED. Discreet shipping. FDA approved (we claim).

Order today and save up to 80%!
www.canada-pharma-direct.biz

To stop emails reply REMOVE.
redFlags: Selling prescription medication without a prescription is illegal
Fake FDA approval claim — 'we claim' is a tell
Extremely low prices indicate counterfeit or dangerous drugs
Sender uses a .biz domain
Unsolicited pharmaceutical advertising
emails.hint: Purchasing prescription medication without a valid prescription is illegal and dangerous. These medications are often counterfeit.

emails.subject: Mom Earns $8,000/Month Stuffing Envelopes From Home!
emails.body:
SHOCKING: Stay-at-home mom reveals how she earns $8,000 every month from her kitchen table!

No experience needed. No boss. Work your own hours.

All you do is stuff envelopes and mail them! Companies pay TOP DOLLAR for this simple task.

Get our starter kit for just $29.99:
www.wfh-weekly-offers.net/starterkit

ACT NOW — Only 47 starter kits left!

Unsubscribe: reply with REMOVE.
redFlags: Unrealistic income claims — $8,000 per month stuffing envelopes
Requires upfront payment for a 'starter kit'
Fake scarcity — 'only 47 kits left'
Classic work-at-home pyramid scheme pattern
Unsolicited job offer
emails.hint: Work-from-home schemes that require an upfront payment are almost always scams. Legitimate employers never charge you to start working.

emails.subject: This Coin Is About to 10X — Get In Before Tuesday
emails.body:
CRYPTO ALERT — URGENT!

Our insider sources have identified a micro-cap coin that is about to EXPLODE 1,000% before Tuesday.

This is a once-in-a-decade opportunity. The whales are quietly accumulating. Once the news breaks, it will be too late.

Buy MOONX token now at cryptowealth-insider.biz/buy before the window closes.

Disclaimer: This is not financial advice. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Unsubscribe: remove@cryptowealth-insider.biz
redFlags: Unverifiable 'insider' claims about price increases
Classic pump-and-dump scheme language
Artificial urgency — 'before Tuesday'
Contradictory disclaimer after making specific predictions
Unsolicited investment advice
emails.hint: Cryptocurrency pump-and-dump schemes use insider language and urgency to get you to buy an asset the scammer already holds — when you buy, they sell.

emails.subject: You've Been Selected for a $500 Amazon Gift Card!
emails.body:
SELECTED CUSTOMER!

Amazon.com is rewarding loyal customers with a $500 gift card as part of our 10th Anniversary celebration!

You've been selected! Claim your card in 3 easy steps:
1. Click the link below
2. Complete a short 2-minute survey
3. Enter your shipping address

CLAIM NOW: http://amazon-rewards-center.net/claim

Offer expires in 24 hours. Limited to the first 100 respondents.
redFlags: Amazon does not randomly send gift cards to customers by email
Sender domain is 'amazon-rewards-center.net' — not amazon.com
Fake scarcity — 'first 100 respondents'
Survey step is used to collect personal data
24-hour expiration creates false urgency
emails.hint: Legitimate Amazon promotions come from amazon.com. Free gift card offers that require surveys are data collection scams.

emails.subject: Exclusive: $1,000 Welcome Bonus — Claim Before Midnight!
emails.body:
EXCLUSIVE OFFER FOR YOU!

Vegas Palace Online Casino is giving NEW members a $1,000 welcome bonus — absolutely FREE!

Deposit just $20 and we'll match it 50x to give you $1,000 in casino credits.

CLAIM BONUS: vegaspalace-online.biz/welcome

Terms: 60x wagering requirement on bonus funds. Withdrawal restricted for 90 days. Valid for 24 hours only.

Unsubscribe: opt-out@vegaspalace-online.biz
redFlags: Unsolicited gambling advertisement
60x wagering requirement makes the bonus nearly impossible to cash out
90-day withdrawal restriction buried in fine print
Sender uses a .biz domain
Artificial midnight deadline
emails.hint: Online casino bonuses with very high wagering requirements are designed so you statistically cannot withdraw. Always read the terms carefully.

emails.subject: Refinance Your Mortgage — Rates as Low as 1.9% — Act Today!
emails.body:
HOMEOWNERS — THIS IS URGENT!

Mortgage rates have dropped to a 40-year low. If you have a home loan over $100,000, you may qualify to save $400-$900/month!

Take 60 seconds to check your rate:
www.quickloans-refinance-now.biz

No credit check. No obligation. Approval in minutes.

This offer is only available through March 20th.

To opt out: unsubscribe@quickloans-refinance-now.biz
redFlags: Unsolicited financial offer sent to mass recipients
1.9% rate claim is unrealistically low
'No credit check' is a red flag for predatory lending
Sender uses a .biz domain with a hyphenated URL
Short deadline pressures quick decisions on major financial choices
emails.hint: Legitimate mortgage lenders do not solicit through mass email. 'No credit check' loans typically come with extremely high hidden fees.

emails.subject: Madam Rosa Sees Great Danger in Your Future — Read This Now
emails.body:
Dear Friend,

Madam Rosa has had a powerful vision concerning YOU specifically. The stars indicate a period of great danger and financial loss unless you act.

Your FREE personal reading reveals:
- A betrayal by someone close to you
- A financial opportunity you must not miss
- The one decision that will change everything

Claim your FREE reading (valued at $199):
www.psychicnation-free.biz/reading

This vision was meant for you alone. Do not ignore it.

Unsubscribe: remove@psychicnation-free.biz
redFlags: Vague predictions that could apply to anyone
Creates fear — 'great danger' — to provoke action
Claims to be personal but is a mass email
'Free' reading valued at $199 implies a future upsell
Sender uses a .biz domain
emails.hint: Psychic spam uses vague fear-based predictions that seem personal but are sent to millions. The 'free' reading always leads to paid upsells.
